metrics.narrative: Buyer articulated operational pain but no quantified business case. On 25 September 2025 Nicole stated 'we do not have a sourcing tool at all… we have a very manual intake process… everything has been very manual' and described contracts living in Jagger only, with no integration to sourcing or procurement. She confirmed two open vacancies (contracts specialist, facilities sourcing specialist), signaling team under-resourced and workload pressure. Pain owner is Director of Procurement (the relevant stakeholder, not yet engaged). No quantified impact mentioned: no cycle-time reduction, cost savings target, error-rate improvement, or headcount-efficiency metric. Buyer did reference 'budgets are limited in higher ed… a lot of it comes down to our budget' and asked where Zycus pricing falls versus Coupa/SAP/Ariba/Jagger, but this is a cost concern, not a value-case metric. Gap: no ROI, no savings projection, no efficiency gain quantified. The 13 July meeting with the Director of Procurement and the relevant stakeholder is the activation point: quantify the pain (e.g. how many sourcing events per year are run manually today? what is the cycle time? what is the cost of the two open vacancies and manual workarounds?) and co-build a business case with the relevant stakeholder anchored to her priorities (sourcing-tool stand-up in 12–18 months, budget constraints, AI/Merlin differentiation).

champion.narrative: Nicole Lanoue (Grants and Finance Manager, Evaluator contact role on opp) is a developing champion with directional advocacy but no demonstrated active championship. On 25 September 2025 she engaged in a 58-minute discovery call, articulated pain and fit across iSource/eProcurement/iContract/Merlin, expressed strong interest in AI ('I'm very intrigued by the Merlin piece'), and committed to brief the relevant stakeholder on Zycus and determine next steps. She also volunteered that Mike, a Tufts hire from CBRE who used Zycus for 5 years, is an internal reference and potential ally. These are positive signals. However, after the discovery call Nicole did not follow up for 8.5 months (25 September 2025 to 4 June 2026 rep re-engagement), suggesting her advocacy cooled, she deprioritized, or internal blockers (budget, the relevant stakeholder ambivalence, hiring distractions) stalled momentum. When rep re-engaged 4 June, Nicole responded positively 5 June and agreed to 13 July meeting, indicating she is still engaged but not driving the deal proactively. No evidence of active advocacy: no introductions made (the relevant stakeholder and Director of Procurement not engaged until 13 July), no internal business case built, no competitor intel shared, no urgency created. Access to EB and DM: Nicole has direct access (works with the relevant stakeholder, can schedule Director of Procurement), but has not yet activated that access to move the deal forward. Champion strength: developing, not strong. Risk: single-threaded (Nicole is only buyer contact on opp), and if she leaves or deprioritizes, deal stalls. Mitigation: on 13 July, activate Mike (Tufts internal reference, former Zycus user) as a second champion to create redundancy, and assess whether Director of Procurement or the relevant stakeholder can be converted into active sponsors.

sources: Avoma discovery call 25 September 2025, Nicole Lanoue (status quo described, AI overlay option explored, Coupa/Ariba pricing benchmarked, Jagger incumbent named)
Salesforce Competitors__c field: Coupa, SAP Ariba
competition.narrative: Primary competitor is status quo / do-nothing, confirmed on 25 September 2025 when buyer described current state (PeopleSoft P2P, Unimarket marketplace, Jagger contracts, manual intake and sourcing) and explored two alternatives: full platform consolidation (Zycus) vs AI overlay on existing stack ('do we wanna consolidate systems, do we want to use AI to almost have a wrapper?'). This is the real competition: inertia and the AI-overlay option, which would avoid rip-and-replace cost and risk. Buyer also named Coupa and SAP Ariba as pricing benchmarks on 25 September: 'I've talked to Coupa before… the price tag is wild… Jagger is pretty expensive too,' and asked where Zycus falls versus them. Rep positioned Zycus as lower-to-mid market, competitive or slightly below Coupa. Coupa and SAP Ariba are in the consideration set (Salesforce Competitors__c field confirms), but buyer has not engaged them recently (no mention of active Coupa/Ariba eval, demos, or RFP). Jagger is contracts-only incumbent, not a full-platform competitor, but buyer may choose to keep Jagger and only add sourcing/intake tools (narrower scope, lower spend). How we win: (1) AI/Merlin differentiation: buyer is 'very intrigued' by Merlin for sourcing negotiation, which Coupa/Ariba/Jagger do not offer at this level. Make Merlin the centerpiece on 13 July demo and tie it to the buyer's AI exploration (Merlin IS the AI overlay on their sourcing process, no custom dev required). (2) Modular pricing to fit budget: position iSource + eProcurement + Merlin as Phase 1 (12–18 month sourcing-tool priority) at a price point below Coupa, with iContract as Phase 2 option (evaluate Jagger replacement later), so Tufts can move fast on sourcing without committing to full platform cost up front. (3) Higher-ed sector credibility: buyer asked for percentage of Zycus customer base in higher ed and Zycus focus/roadmap in sector (explicit requirement from 25 September, still unaddressed). Surface higher-ed references and sector-specific roadmap on 13 July to counter any perception Zycus is not a fit for academic procurement. (4) Urgency creation: tie decision to forcing function (two open procurement vacancies creating workload crisis, new fiscal year budget window, sourcing-tool 12–18 month timeline means decision needed by Q3 2026 to hit implementation target).

identify_pain.narrative: Operational pain is confirmed and articulated by Nicole Lanoue (Grants and Finance Manager, Evaluator) on 25 September 2025. Specific pain points: (1) No sourcing tool: 'we do not have a sourcing tool at all,' forcing manual RFx and supplier selection processes. (2) Manual intake: 'we have a very manual intake process,' creating inefficiency and error risk for internal stakeholders submitting procurement requests. (3) Contracts siloed in Jagger with no integration: contracts live in Jagger (contracts-only incumbent) but do not connect to sourcing or procurement, requiring duplicate data entry and limiting visibility. (4) Team under-resourced: two open vacancies (contracts specialist, facilities sourcing specialist) increase workload pressure on existing procurement team and create urgency for automation. Pain owner is Director of Procurement (the relevant stakeholder, scheduled to engage 13 July 2026), who is responsible for procurement function performance and technology roadmap. Nicole also expressed interest in AI/Merlin for sourcing negotiation: 'I'm very intrigued by the Merlin piece… using AI for sourcing and negotiation,' signaling desire to modernize and gain competitive advantage in supplier negotiations. No financial pain quantified (cost of manual processes, savings opportunity, risk exposure from non-compliant spend), but operational pain is clear and confirmed. The 13 July meeting should quantify the pain with the Director of Procurement: What is the volume of manual sourcing events per year? What is the cycle time today vs target? What is the cost (FTE burden, error/rework rate, maverick spend) of not having a sourcing tool and automated intake?

decision_criteria.narrative: Buyer articulated evaluation priorities on 25 September 2025 but no formal RFP, scorecard, or documented criteria exist. Nicole stated Tufts needs (1) sourcing tool (highest priority, 12–18 month timeline), (2) intake automation (currently manual), (3) contract management (Jagger incumbent, evaluate replacement vs keep), and (4) AI/Merlin capability for sourcing negotiation ('I'm very intrigued by the Merlin piece… using AI for sourcing and negotiation'). Buyer mentioned exploring two strategies: full platform consolidation vs AI overlay on existing stack ('do we wanna consolidate systems, do we want to use AI to almost have a wrapper?'), which is a strategic decision criterion the relevant stakeholder must resolve. No weighted scorecard, no test-case plan, no reference-check list, no InfoSec requirements documented. Gap: formal criteria not locked, RFP vs sole-source decision not made, AI-overlay alternative not ruled out. The 13 July meeting with Director of Procurement and the relevant stakeholder is the gate to confirm: Is this sole-source or RFP? What are the must-haves vs nice-to-haves for sourcing, intake, contracts, AI? How does Tufts weight sector expertise, pricing, ease-of-use, integration (PeopleSoft P2P, Unimarket, Teams for Merlin)? What decision timeline (sourcing-only by when? full platform by when?)? Lock the criteria and the process on that call.

north_star_verdict.math: Deal created 21 November 2025, qualified same day. Close date 28 October 2026 (121 days out). Time in Qualified: 220 days. No submission-date fields populated, so stage-velocity read relies on field history. Last buyer touch: discovery call 25 September 2025 (278 days ago). Rep re-engaged 4 June 2026 via email, buyer responded 5 June, meeting scheduled for 13 July 2026 (14 days from today, 29 June 2026). Formal eval/shortlist/vendor-selected gates not reached. To close in 121 days from Qualified requires demo + RFI/RFP + shortlist + commercials + negotiation + contract in ~17 weeks (aggressive for higher-ed enterprise motion, which typically runs 12–15 months). Buyer gave conflicting timelines: sourcing tool desired in 12–18 months (maps to Q3 2026–Q1 2027, aligns with close date) but broader platform evolution over 1–3 years, and stated 'I'm not quite sure what our next step should be' on 25 September 2025, signaling ambivalence. No forward slips recorded. Required pace: lock demo + EB engagement + pricing + RFP scope by mid-August, shortlist by mid-September, commercials by early October to hit 28 October close. Current trajectory (meeting scheduled 13 July, no demo yet delivered) puts deal 4–6 weeks behind required pace.
evidence: Rep re-engaged 4 June 2026 after 8.5-month silence; buyer replied 5 June confirming interest and availability; meeting scheduled 13 July 2026 with Director of Procurement and the relevant stakeholder for intake/sourcing demo (Next Step 29 June 2026, LastActivityDate 13 July 2026).

Discovery call 25 September 2025: buyer articulated pain (manual intake, no sourcing tool, contract mgmt in Jagger only), interest in AI/Merlin for sourcing negotiation, and directional fit across iSource/eProcurement/iContract/Merlin, but deferred module prioritization to the relevant stakeholder and gave conflicting timelines (12–18 months for sourcing, 1–3 years for full platform).

Pipeline forecast is defensible: deal is engaged (meeting in 14 days) but early-stage (Qualified, no demo, no EB engagement, no pricing, no validated timeline/budget), so Pipeline is the honest category. Upside exists if 13 July meeting accelerates (EB commits, budget confirmed, RFP/sole-source decision locked), but insufficient evidence today for Best Case.
north_star_verdict.headline: Buyer meeting scheduled 13 July (14 days out) after 8.5-month gap, but deal is 121 days from close in Qualified stage with no demo delivered, no pricing, no EB engagement, and no validated timeline or budget. Directional buyer interest confirmed 25 September 2025, but internal priority unclear and decision process unmapped.

flags: Close the higher-ed fit exposure and savings-tracking wedge on 13 July. Buyer explicitly asked for Zycus higher-ed customer percentage, sector roadmap, and integrated savings-management capability (the 'freaking miracle') on 25 September 2025; rep committed to deliver by 26 September 2025 afternoon but no evidence it was provided (9 months overdue). This is a named buyer concern ('higher ed is a little unique… where your industry focus is… can be notable') and an unmet commitment that weakens trust and competitive positioning versus Jagger (higher-ed incumbent) and Coupa (buyer familiarity). On 13 July, lead with: (a) Zycus higher-ed customer count, marquee university logos, and sector-specific roadmap investments to prove sector commitment; (b) live demo of savings-management integration (sourcing award auto-populating cost savings) to deliver the wedge the buyer desperately wants and Coupa/Ariba do not offer out-of-box. Closing this gap converts the buyer's budget/fit concerns into a Zycus differentiator and builds credibility for the broader demo.

Multi-thread beyond Nicole to de-risk single-contact dependency and secure power-role engagement. Nicole Lanoue (Grants and Finance Manager, Evaluator) is the only engaged buyer contact (1 contact role on opp, participated in 25 September 2025 call); the relevant stakeholder (Economic Buyer) and Director of Procurement (decision-process participant, named in 13 July Next Step) have not yet appeared on a call, and the 8 January 2026 business-case sync was not recorded (attendees unknown). Single-thread risk is high: if Nicole deprioritizes, the deal stalls. On 13 July (first confirmed CPO touchpoint), validate Nizrin's budget authority, timeline, and module prioritization; ask Nicole to introduce Director of Procurement and other evaluators (IT/InfoSec for Teams/Merlin integration, Finance for savings-case validation); and activate the Tufts hire Mike (came from CBRE, used Zycus for sourcing + contracts for 5 years, called it 'slick'—per Nicole's 25 September statement) as an internal reference and potential champion by asking Nicole to loop him into a follow-up call to share his Zycus experience and validate the platform's enterprise delivery. Multi-threading now (while deal is early and engagement is live) prevents single-point-of-failure risk and accelerates decision-process mapping.

Lead with the agentic AI wedge (Merlin + ANA) to differentiate from Coupa/Ariba and convert exploratory interest into committed evaluation. Buyer is exploring AI overlay on existing systems ('do we want to use AI to almost have a wrapper or GUI layer?') and is ambivalent about next steps ('I'm not quite sure what our next step should be'), creating high inertia/do-nothing risk. The buyer's unique pain—decentralized non-specialist buyers ('people who do the purchasing are not dedicated to purchasing… they just need the things to arrive') and maverick spend—is a perfect fit for Zycus Merlin guided-buying chatbot (lives in Teams, natural-language procurement, guides users to approved suppliers/contracts) and ANA autonomous tail-spend negotiation (addresses the low-volume, low-dollar purchases resource-constrained procurement teams cannot oversee). Coupa and Ariba have AI features but not this depth of agentic, embedded-workflow AI maturity (Zycus Merlin launched 2017, 8+ years mature). On 13 July, make Merlin + ANA the centerpiece of the intake/sourcing demo: show Merlin chatbot in a Teams environment procuring a monitor (the rep's 25 September example) with guided buying enforcing approved suppliers, then show ANA autonomously negotiating a tail-spend RFQ. Contrast this with Coupa's bolt-on AI and the status-quo fragmentation, positioning Zycus as the AI-first platform that delivers the unification the buyer is exploring PLUS the source-to-pay backbone they need. This is the single highest-leverage move to create urgency, differentiate from competition, and convert the 12–18 month sourcing-tool interest into a committed evaluation with a realistic close path.

Mitigation: on 13 July, activate Mike (Tufts internal reference, former CBRE Zycus user) as second champion and assess Director of Procurement and the relevant stakeholder for active sponsorship.

No demo delivered, 121 days to close. Discovery call 25 September 2025 established directional fit, but no product demonstration, no use-case validation, no Merlin AI demo (buyer 'very intrigued' but has not seen it). 13 July meeting is first demo (intake + sourcing scope per Next Step). For enterprise higher-ed deal, demo → RFP → shortlist → commercials → negotiation → contract typically requires 12–15 months; this deal has 121 days (4 months). Mitigation: deliver high-impact Merlin demo 13 July, lock sole-source decision if possible (skip RFP to compress timeline), and validate realistic close date with the relevant stakeholder (if 12–18 month sourcing-tool timeline is real, close date should be Q1–Q2 2027, not October 2026).

No Economic Buyer engagement, no budget validated. the relevant stakeholder (EB per Next Step 29 June) scheduled for first touchpoint 13 July, but no prior engagement, no commercial/pricing conversation, no budget range confirmed. Buyer stated 'budgets are limited in higher ed… a lot of it comes down to our budget' and Coupa 'price tag is wild' (25 September 2025), signaling cost sensitivity, but $100k ACV opp amount not validated against Tufts budget. Mitigation: on 13 July, confirm the relevant stakeholder's budget authority, validate budget range (can Tufts afford $100k ACV or does it need modular/phased pricing?), and map approval chain (CFO sign-off? committee?).

Three explicit requirements from 25 September 2025 still unaddressed: (1) percentage of Zycus customer base in higher ed + Zycus higher-ed focus/roadmap, (2) savings-management capability and integration with sourcing awards, (3) demo options (scheduled 13 July, not yet delivered). Rep committed to send follow-up by 26 September 2025 afternoon with items 1 and 2, but no evidence follow-up sent. Mitigation: deliver all three on 13 July meeting (higher-ed references/roadmap in deck, savings-mgmt explanation in demo, intake/sourcing demo tailored to Tufts needs).

No quantified business case or ROI. Operational pain confirmed (no sourcing tool, manual intake, two open vacancies, Jagger contracts siloed) but no quantified impact (cycle-time reduction, cost savings, headcount efficiency, error-rate improvement, maverick-spend reduction). Mitigation: on 13 July, co-build business case with Director of Procurement and the relevant stakeholder: quantify volume of manual sourcing events per year, current cycle time vs target, FTE burden of manual processes, cost of two open vacancies, and project ROI from Zycus automation (time savings, cost avoidance, faster time-to-award).
best_practice_check.summary: Deal is single-threaded (Nicole Lanoue only contact on opp, the relevant stakeholder and Director of Procurement not yet engaged), no demo delivered, no EB engagement, no pricing/budget validated, and 121 days from close in Qualified stage after 8.5-month gap. Meeting scheduled 13 July 2026 (14 days out) is the critical gate to multi-thread, lock decision process and timeline, validate budget, and deliver Merlin differentiation. Three explicit requirements from 25 September 2025 still unaddressed (higher-ed customer base %, savings-mgmt explanation, demo). Champion (Nicole) is developing, not strong, and at risk of deprioritization (8.5-month gap evidence). No quantified business case or ROI.

competitive_position.summary: Primary competition is status quo / do-nothing (PeopleSoft P2P + Unimarket + Jagger contracts + potential AI overlay exploration) and budget constraints. Coupa and SAP Ariba are in consideration set as pricing benchmarks but not actively engaged. Jagger is contracts-only incumbent, replacement vs keep is open question. Zycus positioned as lower-to-mid market pricing (competitive or below Coupa) with AI/Merlin differentiation, but no formal pricing discussion or budget validation has occurred. Wedge is Merlin agentic AI for sourcing negotiation (buyer 'very intrigued'), higher-ed sector fit, and modular pricing to address budget concerns. Risk: AI-overlay alternative (build wrapper on existing stack instead of rip-and-replace) and absolute-dollar budget ceiling may favor do-nothing or sourcing-tool-only narrow scope over full platform.

value.flag: Lead with the agentic AI wedge (Merlin + ANA) to differentiate from Coupa/Ariba and convert exploratory interest into committed evaluation. Buyer is exploring AI overlay on existing systems ('do we want to use AI to almost have a wrapper or GUI layer?') and is ambivalent about next steps ('I'm not quite sure what our next step should be'), creating high inertia/do-nothing risk. The buyer's unique pain—decentralized non-specialist buyers ('people who do the purchasing are not dedicated to purchasing… they just need the things to arrive') and maverick spend—is a perfect fit for Zycus Merlin guided-buying chatbot (lives in Teams, natural-language procurement, guides users to approved suppliers/contracts) and ANA autonomous tail-spend negotiation (addresses the low-volume, low-dollar purchases resource-constrained procurement teams cannot oversee). Coupa and Ariba have AI features but not this depth of agentic, embedded-workflow AI maturity (Zycus Merlin launched 2017, 8+ years mature). On 13 July, make Merlin + ANA the centerpiece of the intake/sourcing demo: show Merlin chatbot in a Teams environment procuring a monitor (the rep's 25 September example) with guided buying enforcing approved suppliers, then show ANA autonomously negotiating a tail-spend RFQ. Contrast this with Coupa's bolt-on AI and the status-quo fragmentation, positioning Zycus as the AI-first platform that delivers the unification the buyer is exploring PLUS the source-to-pay backbone they need. This is the single highest-leverage move to create urgency, differentiate from competition, and convert the 12–18 month sourcing-tool interest into a committed evaluation with a realistic close path.
